section.title: The Levites and Their Duties
section.sectionRationale: Immediately after the tribes are counted and arranged around the tabernacle, the narrative turns to the priestly line and the tribe of Levi, explaining why the Levites replace Israel's firstborn sons and detailing their clans, numbers, and duties, the theological center beneath the census seam.
section.thesis: God takes the Levites for Himself in place of every Israelite firstborn, redeemed at the Exodus, organizing them by clan around the tabernacle to serve, and requiring a redemption price for the firstborn who exceed the Levites in number, showing that access to His holy dwelling is only by His own gracious provision, not human initiative.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the sons of Aaron and the tragedy of Nadab and Abihu; the Levites given to Aaron to assist the priesthood; the three Levite clans, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, numbered and positioned with their duties; and the redemption of the firstborn who outnumber the Levites.

units.label: The line of Aaron
units.note: Nadab and Abihu die before the LORD

verses.text: This is the account of Aaron and Moses at the time the LORD spoke with Moses on Mount Sinai.
These are the names of the sons of Aaron: Nadab the firstborn, then Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
These were Aaron's sons, the anointed priests, who were ordained to serve as priests.

Nadab and Abihu, however, died in the presence of the LORD when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD in the Wilderness of Sinai. And since they had no sons, only Eleazar and Ithamar served as priests during the lifetime of their father Aaron.

units.label: The Levites given to the priests
units.note: taken in place of every firstborn

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses,
“Bring the tribe of Levi and present them to Aaron the priest to assist him.
They are to perform duties for him and for the whole congregation before the Tent of Meeting, attending to the service of the tabernacle.
They shall take care of all the furnishings of the Tent of Meeting and fulfill obligations for the Israelites by attending to the service of the tabernacle.
Assign the Levites to Aaron and his sons; they have been given exclusively to him from among the Israelites.
So you shall appoint Aaron and his sons to carry out the duties of the priesthood; but any outsider who approaches the tabernacle must be put to death.”
Again the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
“Behold, I have taken the Levites from among the children of Israel in place of every firstborn Israelite from the womb. The Levites belong to Me,
for all the firstborn are Mine. On the day I struck down every firstborn in the land of Egypt, I consecrated to Myself all the firstborn in Israel, both man and beast. They are Mine; I am the LORD.”

units.label: The three Levite clans numbered and positioned

verses.text: Then the LORD spoke to Moses in the Wilderness of Sinai, saying,
“Number the Levites by their families and clans. You are to count every male a month old or more.”
So Moses numbered them according to the word of the LORD, as he had been commanded.
These were the sons of Levi by name: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
These were the names of the sons of Gershon by their clans: Libni and Shimei.
The sons of Kohath by their clans were Amram, Izhar, Hebron, and Uzziel.
And the sons of Merari by their clans were Mahli and Mushi. These were the clans of the Levites, according to their families.
From Gershon came the Libnite clan and the Shimeite clan; these were the Gershonite clans.
The number of all the males a month old or more was 7,500.
The Gershonite clans were to camp on the west, behind the tabernacle,
and the leader of the families of the Gershonites was Eliasaph son of Lael.
The duties of the Gershonites at the Tent of Meeting were the tabernacle and tent, its covering, the curtain for the entrance to the Tent of Meeting,
the curtains of the courtyard, the curtain for the entrance to the courtyard that surrounds the tabernacle and altar, and the cords, all the service for these items.
From Kohath came the clans of the Amramites, the Izharites, the Hebronites, and the Uzzielites; these were the clans of the Kohathites.
The number of all the males a month old or more was 8,600. They were responsible for the duties of the sanctuary.
The clans of the Kohathites were to camp on the south side of the tabernacle,
and the leader of the families of the Kohathites was Elizaphan son of Uzziel.
Their duties were the ark, the table, the lampstand, the altars, the articles of the sanctuary used with them, and the curtain, all the service for these items.
The chief of the leaders of the Levites was Eleazar son of Aaron the priest; he oversaw those responsible for the duties of the sanctuary.
From Merari came the clans of the Mahlites and Mushites; these were the Merarite clans.
The number of all the males a month old or more was 6,200.
The leader of the families of the Merarites was Zuriel son of Abihail; they were to camp on the north side of the tabernacle.
The duties assigned to the sons of Merari were the tabernacle's frames, crossbars, posts, bases, and all its equipment, all the service for these items,
as well as the posts of the surrounding courtyard with their bases, tent pegs, and ropes.

Moses, Aaron, and Aaron's sons were to camp to the east of the tabernacle, toward the sunrise, before the Tent of Meeting. They were to perform the duties of the sanctuary as a service on behalf of the Israelites; but any outsider who approached the sanctuary was to be put to death.
The total number of Levites that Moses and Aaron counted by their clans at the LORD's command, including all the males a month old or more, was 22,000.

units.label: Redeeming the firstborn
units.note: a payment for those who exceed the Levites

verses.text: Then the LORD said to Moses, “Number every firstborn male of the Israelites a month old or more, and list their names.
You are to take the Levites for Me, I am the LORD, in place of all the firstborn of Israel, and the livestock of the Levites in place of all the firstborn of the livestock of the Israelites.”
So Moses numbered all the firstborn of the Israelites, as the LORD had commanded him.
The total number of the firstborn males a month old or more, listed by name, was 22,273.
Again the LORD spoke to Moses, saying,
“Take the Levites in place of all the firstborn of Israel, and the livestock of the Levites in place of their livestock. The Levites belong to Me; I am the LORD.
To {{redeem|redeem}} the 273 firstborn Israelites who outnumber the Levites,
you are to collect five shekels for each one, according to the sanctuary shekel of twenty gerahs.
Give the money to Aaron and his sons as the redemption price for the excess among the Israelites.”
So Moses collected the redemption money from those in excess of the number redeemed by the Levites.
He collected the money from the firstborn of the Israelites: 1,365 shekels, according to the sanctuary shekel.
And Moses gave the redemption money to Aaron and his sons in obedience to the word of the LORD, just as the LORD had commanded him.

redeem.gloss: To redeem, ransom, or buy back at a price. Applied here to the 273 firstborn Israelites who exceed the number of Levites, showing that even substitution sometimes leaves a remainder that must be covered by an additional payment.

basic.context: Chapter 3 explains why the Levites exist as a distinct tribe: God claims every Israelite firstborn as His own from the Exodus night, but takes the Levites instead, organizing them into three clans with specific duties in transporting and guarding the tabernacle.
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical instruction establishing Israel's priestly and Levitical structure, not merely genealogy. The chapter's repeated emphasis on holiness and the danger of unauthorized approach, seen already in Nadab and Abihu's death, shows that nearness to God is a gift requiring careful, God-given order.
basic.originalLanguages: The firstborn who exceed the number of Levites must be padah, redeemed, bought back with silver, showing that even substitution has a further cost when the numbers do not exactly match. The whole chapter turns on this logic of substitution and redemption rather than raw headcount.
basic.typology: The Levites standing in the place of Israel's firstborn, redeemed at the cost of silver, anticipates the greater substitution of Christ, who is Himself the true Firstborn, redeeming His people not with silver but with His own blood, and who now serves as the true and final Priest.
basic.christ: Where the Levites substitute for Israel's firstborn and a redemption price is paid in silver, Christ is Himself the firstborn over all creation, Colossians 1:15, who redeems His people not with silver or gold but with His own precious blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19, becoming both the substitute and the price paid.
basic.god: God's holiness is shown starkly in the death of Nadab and Abihu for unauthorized fire, verse 4, yet His grace is shown just as clearly in providing the Levites so ordinary Israelites need not risk death for approaching the sanctuary carelessly. Holiness and provision for access stand together throughout.
basic.crossReferences: Exodus 13:1-2 first claims every firstborn for the LORD at the Passover. Colossians 1:15 calls Christ the firstborn over all creation. 1 Peter 1:18-19 describes redemption by Christ's precious blood rather than silver or gold. Hebrews 7 shows Christ as the priest who fulfills the Levitical order.
basic.oneStory: God claims the firstborn as His own from the night of the Exodus and provides the Levites as a substitute, redeemed by a price, so His people can approach Him safely. That pattern of substitution and redemption finds its fulfillment in Christ, the true firstborn, who redeems His people at the cost of His own blood and opens permanent access to God.

context.literary: This chapter grounds the Levites' unique status established briefly in chapter 1 verses 47 to 53, explaining in full why they are set apart, and it prepares for chapter 4's detailed assignment of specific transport duties to each clan.
context.historical: The death of Nadab and Abihu, recalled from Leviticus 10, casts a serious shadow over this whole chapter: serving in God's presence is a privilege that carries real danger for those who approach carelessly, a lesson the newly organized Levites must take seriously.
context.canonical: The principle that the firstborn belongs to the LORD threads through the whole Pentateuch from the Passover onward, and the Levites' substitutionary role here becomes a recurring pattern in Israel's theology of redemption, culminating in the New Testament's application of firstborn language to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To explain and legitimate the unique, substitutionary role of the Levites in place of Israel's firstborn, organizing them by clan for specific service, and to underline the seriousness of unauthorized approach to God's holy things.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes a specific, historical arrangement for Israel's tabernacle worship; it does not prescribe an ongoing Levitical order for the New Testament church, which now has direct access to God through Christ, the great High Priest. Its abiding principle is that access to God's presence is always by His gracious provision.

debates.question: Why did Nadab and Abihu die for offering unauthorized fire, mentioned again here in verse 4?

views.label: A direct act of disobedience meriting judgment
views.case: Leviticus 10:1-2 describes them offering fire God had not commanded, treated as a serious breach of the exact worship God prescribed for His holy presence.

views.label: A warning example for the newly organized priesthood
views.case: Its repetition here, just as the Levites are formally organized, may function specifically to warn the new priestly workforce of the stakes involved in serving at the tabernacle.

debates.bearing: Either way, the episode underlines the chapter's central concern: approaching God's holy things is never casual, and the Levites exist partly to manage that danger safely for the rest of Israel.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on padah, to redeem or ransom, applied to the 273 firstborn Israelites who exceed the number of Levites and must be bought back with five shekels each. The logic of substitution, one group standing in the place of another, and of redemption, a price paid to release what is owed, runs through the entire passage.

typology.type: The Levites standing in the place of Israel's firstborn
typology.fulfillment: Christ, the true firstborn, Colossians 1:15, who stands in the place of His people before God.

typology.type: Redemption of the firstborn by a price of silver
typology.fulfillment: Redemption of God's people at the far greater cost of Christ's own precious blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19.

study.christ: The Levites substitute for Israel's firstborn, and a redemption price in silver covers those who exceed their number; both pictures converge in Christ, who is Himself the firstborn over all creation, Colossians 1:15, and who redeems His people not with silver or gold but with His own precious blood, 1 Peter 1:18-19, becoming both the true substitute and the price paid in full.
study.god: God's holiness and grace hold together in this chapter: His holiness is evident in the death of Nadab and Abihu for careless worship, yet His grace provides the Levites precisely so ordinary Israelites do not have to bear that same risk in approaching His sanctuary. He makes a way for His people to draw near safely.

crossReferences.note: First establishes that every firstborn belongs to the LORD, the foundation this chapter builds on.
Calls Christ the firstborn over all creation, the ultimate fulfillment of the firstborn theme running through this chapter.
Describes believers redeemed not with silver or gold but with the precious blood of Christ, surpassing the redemption price paid here.
Shows Christ as the permanent priest who fulfills and surpasses the Levitical priesthood this chapter organizes.

study.oneStory: God claims the firstborn as His own from the Exodus and provides the Levites as a substitute, redeemed by a price, so His people can approach Him without fear. That pattern of substitution and redemption reaches its fulfillment in Christ, the true firstborn, who redeems His people at the cost of His own blood and opens lasting access to God.

quiz.q: Why does God take the Levites for Himself instead of Israel's firstborn sons?

options.text: Because He had already claimed every firstborn in Israel on the night He struck the firstborn of Egypt, and now takes the Levites in their place.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 and 45 explain this directly.

options.text: Because the Levites requested this role, seeking greater status.
options.note: The text presents this as God's own initiative, not a Levite request.

options.text: Because Israel's firstborn had all died in the wilderness.
options.note: The firstborn are alive and are counted in this very chapter, verse 43.

options.text: No reason is given for taking the Levites.
options.note: Verses 12 to 13 and 45 state the reason explicitly.

quiz.q: What happened to Nadab and Abihu, and why is it recalled in this chapter?

options.text: They died before the LORD for offering unauthorized fire, a sober reminder as the Levites are formally organized for service.
options.note: Verse 4 states this plainly.

options.text: They were exiled from Israel for disobedience.
options.note: The text says they died, not that they were exiled.

options.text: They became the first high priests after Aaron.
options.note: Eleazar and Ithamar served as priests instead, verse 4, not Nadab and Abihu.

options.text: The text gives no information about them here.
options.note: Verse 4 recounts their deaths directly.

quiz.q: How were the 273 extra firstborn Israelites, beyond the number of Levites, dealt with?

options.text: They were redeemed with five shekels each, according to the sanctuary shekel.
options.note: Verses 46 to 47 state this redemption price exactly.

options.text: They were also made Levites.
options.note: The text specifies a monetary redemption, not additional Levite status.

options.text: They were simply forgiven with no payment required.
options.note: A specific redemption price is explicitly required.

options.text: The text does not address the excess firstborn.
options.note: Verses 46 to 51 address this exact situation.

quiz.q: Which three clans made up the tribe of Levi?

options.text: Gershon, Kohath, and Merari.
options.note: Verse 17 names these as the sons of Levi and their clans.

options.text: Aaron, Eleazar, and Ithamar.
options.note: These are priestly individuals within Aaron's family, not the three Levite clans.

options.text: Reuben, Simeon, and Levi.
options.note: These are three of Jacob's sons, not the clans descending from Levi himself.

options.text: The text names no specific clans.
options.note: Verse 17 names the three clans directly.

quiz.q: What was the consequence for an outsider who approached the tabernacle without authorization?

options.text: Death, stated explicitly for anyone without proper standing who approaches.
options.note: Verses 10 and 38 state this consequence directly.

options.text: A fine paid in silver.
options.note: That describes the firstborn redemption price, a separate matter from unauthorized approach.

options.text: Temporary exile from the camp for seven days.
options.note: That penalty pattern appears elsewhere, for example with skin disease, not for unauthorized approach here.

options.text: No penalty is mentioned for unauthorized approach.
options.note: Verses 10 and 38 state the penalty explicitly.

questions: God claimed the firstborn, then provided the Levites as a substitute so ordinary Israelites would not risk death approaching His holy things. Where do you take for granted the access to God that Christ purchased at great cost?

Nadab and Abihu's death is recalled as a sober warning even as the Levites are organized for joyful service. How do you hold together reverence for God's holiness with confidence in approaching Him through Christ?

The redemption price for the extra firstborn shows that even substitution sometimes required an additional payment to be exact. How does knowing Christ's redemption leaves nothing outstanding change how you carry guilt or unfinished business with God?
Each Levite clan, Gershon, Kohath, and Merari, had a distinct and necessary task. What specific task has God given you in His household that only you are positioned to carry?
The Levites belonged to God in a special way, set apart for His service among His people. In what areas of your life do you resist being set apart, wanting to blend in rather than serve distinctly?

translationNotes.note: The Levites belong to Me echoes the claim on the firstborn first made in Exodus 13:1-2, tying the two chapters together in a single theology of substitution.
The total, 22,000 Levites, is 273 fewer than the 22,273 firstborn Israelites counted in verse 43, exactly the gap the redemption money in verses 46 to 51 accounts for.
The sanctuary shekel, twenty gerahs, is a fixed standard of weight distinct from ordinary commercial shekels, underlining that this redemption is a sacred transaction.
